Q: Used timing strategy lessons to fix a pattern of panicking and overspending time on hard Quant questions.
Learned to mark a difficult Quant question for review and move on rather than sacrificing the section.
Used the review feature to return to a marked Quant question, recognize the solution, and change the answer.
Turned a strong Quant background into a Q90 by improving pacing rather than only adding content.
V: Focused on strengthening, weakening, and assumption questions because they had been especially difficult as a non-native speaker.
Practiced until identifying premises and eliminating wrong choices became less mentally exhausting.
Used a high-volume question bank and lessons to improve accuracy in the areas covered during a short prep window.
DI: With limited DI prep time, focused primarily on Graph Interpretation.
Used TTP DI questions as a close proxy for real graph questions.
Noted that online official materials included graph/table practice even if the hard-copy book was limited.
overall: Restarted after a long gap with one month before the test and no room to reschedule.
Moved beyond a prior 655 by switching from broad official-guide practice to strategy-focused prep.
Skimmed lessons and practiced as much as possible within the short window.
Used the DI and Quant sections before the break to gauge that the second attempt was going better.
Accepted a compressed prep plan because school, applications, and thesis work made delay impractical.

Q: After a 655 first attempt, focused mostly on Quant before the retake.
Practiced TTP chapter tests and official GMAT questions under real test conditions.
Stopped pausing when stuck and practiced managing panic under time pressure.
Recovered from a first-test Quant derailment caused by getting stuck on question 2.
Used improved mocks to confirm Quant recovery before booking the retake.
V: Followed a structured theory-practice-repeat path until wrong answers became easier to detect.
Improved from feeling weak in Verbal to narrowing most questions to one or two viable choices.
DI: Treated DI as quicker to learn once Quant and Verbal foundations were stronger.
Tried to solve all DI questions and guessed only when confused or running out of time near the end.
For MSR, read the passages and data correctly and wrote down important information so later questions could be answered faster.
Accepted that DI can still score highly with several wrong answers, preferably late in the section.
overall: Started slowly while working, then raised weekly study time after realizing progress was too limited.
Committed to at least ten hours per week and later recommended starting earlier with more consistent hours.
Booked the first exam too soon after finishing theory and later recognized the need for a dedicated mock and review phase.
Took a short break between attempts, then retested after the required wait once mocks reached 715-725.
Scheduled the second test later in the morning and stayed calm through technical issues.

Q: Maintained a strong Quant base but watched for tricky wording in word problems.
After a mock drop caused by a silly easy-question mistake, added focused practice on Quant weak spots.
Used official questions and careful solution review rather than broad unofficial practice.
V: Focused on CR as a major weakness and followed GMAT Intensive material.
Practiced topic by topic, watching lessons first and then completing official-question practice before moving on.
Redid CR questions repeatedly across the prep cycle until language, traps, and reasoning patterns became familiar.
Worked from easy questions to medium and then hard, requiring strong accuracy before increasing difficulty.
Avoided LSAT-style or third-party Verbal late in prep because it habituated the mind to non-GMAT language.
DI: Prioritized DI after a prior attempt where the section could not be completed.
Used official questions only and repeated DI questions many times to internalize tricky wording and options.
Analyzed DI mistakes after every mock and targeted specific weak areas.
Changed section order from DI-first to Verbal, DI, then Quant after realizing DI-first was not ideal.
Initially practiced untimed for understanding, then gradually added timing once comfortable.
overall: Improved from 565 to 685 over six months by focusing on CR and DI rather than rushing another attempt.
Balanced GMAT with social life, fitness, office, and mood management to avoid the negativity of prior cycles.
Used GMAT Tracker spreadsheets to practice and annotate mistakes.
Focused on quality over quantity and deliberate review of why each miss happened.
Made multiple revisions of mistakes and slow questions instead of constantly chasing new material.

Q: Recognized that overconfidence in Quant after an earlier Q90 caused underpreparation for a later attempt.
Kept time checks at questions 7, 13, and 19 to manage Quant pacing.
Used dedicated scratch-pad space for each Quant question to avoid silly errors.
Practiced the hardest available questions so actual mixed difficulty felt smoother.
V: Changed section order to place Verbal first because it required the most mental energy.
Used LSAT RC and CR questions to strengthen elite Verbal performance after exhausting GMAT Club high-difficulty material.
Built hard Verbal sets to make official questions feel more manageable.
Kept time checks at questions 7, 13, and 19 for pacing.
DI: Avoided putting Verbal and DI back-to-back in the old sequence because it drained focus.
Placed DI after the break in the final order because it was hardest to score and benefited from a reset.
Kept DI time checks at questions 5, 10, and 15.
Used dedicated scratch-pad space for DI to reduce careless errors.
overall: After two 665 attempts, reanalyzed both preparation and test-day execution instead of only doing more questions.
Practiced meditation and breathing daily to improve focus and filter distracting thoughts.
Created daily section sets in a new order, Verbal, Quant, DI, using hard questions to build stamina.
Questioned assumptions about strongest sections and best section order.
Used brain games such as Sudoku, crosswords, and spelling games to stay mentally sharp without doom-scrolling.

Q: Learned that an engineering background did not remove the need for careful GMAT Quant execution.
Stopped rushing Quant to build a time bank because misreads were costing points.
Slowed down, read carefully, and solved methodically, leaving fewer questions needing review.
V: For CR, used pre-thinking by asking what would strengthen or weaken the argument before looking at choices.
Moved from reactive answer-choice evaluation to proactive reasoning.
For RC, stopped trying to memorize passages and focused on why the author was making each point.
DI: Initially planned to skip MSR, but abandoned that avoidance strategy when mocks showed it was backfiring.
Recognized that consecutive wrong answers from skipping MSR could be heavily penalized.
Shifted toward attempting solvable DI questions rather than avoiding an entire subtype.
overall: Moved from failed classic-GMAT attempts to a 695 Focus score after seeking structured help.
Used mocks as dress rehearsals for timing and strategy rather than as final judgments of ability.
Worked through anxiety around mocks by focusing on process consistency.
Learned to ask for help and stay resilient through format changes and repeated attempts.

Q: For the retake, focused only on official questions and concept clarity instead of racing the timer.
Moved from easy to medium to hard questions and watched accuracy improve as difficulty increased.
Practiced 30-40 questions daily when possible, with at least a small set on bad days.
Prioritized concepts and accuracy first, believing timing would improve once methods became stronger.
Avoided timed practice when it was creating anxiety and reducing accuracy.
Finished official Quant with substantial time left after accuracy-focused preparation.
overall: Balanced full-time work with GMAT prep, often studying in short sessions before work and before sleep.
Used Magoosh, Official Guide, GMAT Ninja, and GMAT Club videos early, then switched to OG-only practice later.
Took a short break after a disappointing first attempt before restarting.
Mixed practice with mocks and avoided taking continuous mocks to the point of exhaustion.
Treated the 605 to 675 improvement as confidence-building while still aiming higher.
